name: Marquez, Jessica J.
recent_1: Karasinski, J. A., & Marquez, J. J. (2025). Analog Astronaut Self-Scheduling in NASA's HERA Campaign 7. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251370737
recent_2: Marquez, J. J., Karasinski, J. A., Stirling, L., & Lee, J. D. (2025). Space Human Factors Research: NASA's Human Capabilities Assessments for Autonomous Missions. Proceedings of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society Annual Meeting. DOI: 10.1177/10711813251366310
recent_3: Shelat, S., Marquez, J. J., Zheng, J., & Karasinski, J. A. (2024). Collaborative System Usability in Spaceflight Analog Environments through Remote Observations. Applied Sciences, 14(5), 2005. DOI: 10.3390/app14052005
recent_1_synopsis: Reports on how analog astronauts used self-scheduling during Campaign 7 of NASA's Human Exploration Research Analog (HERA), characterizing the volume, timing, and types of crew-driven schedule modifications. The findings inform how much planning autonomy crews can be granted on deep-space missions where ground-support latency makes real-time replanning impractical.
recent_2_synopsis: Synthesizes results from NASA's Human Research Program VNSCOR (HCAAM) portfolio of seven projects probing the human factors barriers to more autonomous crewed missions, spanning workload, decision support, and operations tooling. It frames a research agenda for assessing crew capability to operate with reduced ground control on Moon-to-Mars missions.
recent_3_synopsis: Develops and validates a remote conversation-analysis method to assess the usability of collaborative planning technology used by crews in NASA's HERA analog environment without on-site observers. It demonstrates that remote observation can yield credible usability evidence for spaceflight-analog studies, addressing a practical constraint of isolated, confined missions.
seminal_1: Marquez, J. J., Miller, M. J., Cohen, T., Deliz, I., Lees, D. S., Zheng, J., & Hillenius, S. (2019). Future Needs for Science-Driven Geospatial and Temporal Extravehicular Activity Planning and Execution. Astrobiology, 19(3), 440-461. DOI: 10.1089/ast.2018.1838
seminal_2: Marquez, J. J., Hillenius, S., Kanefsky, B., Zheng, J., Deliz, I., & Reagan, M. (2017). Increasing crew autonomy for long duration exploration missions: Self-scheduling. 2017 IEEE Aerospace Conference, 1-10. DOI: 10.1109/AERO.2017.7943838
seminal_1_synopsis: Articulates the information, geospatial, and temporal planning requirements for science-driven extravehicular activity (EVA) on future planetary surface missions, drawing on analog field campaigns. With approximately 24 citations (Crossref), it is among her most-cited works and is foundational for the design of EVA planning and execution tools that balance science return against operational constraints.
seminal_2_synopsis: Introduces crew self-scheduling as an operational concept for increasing autonomy on long-duration deep-space missions, supported by qualitative evidence from NASA Extreme Environment Mission Operations (NEEMO) analog missions. With approximately 23 citations (Crossref), it is foundational to the Playbook line of research and is widely referenced as an early framing of crew-autonomous planning for exploration missions.
expertise_overview: Space human factors and human-systems integration for spaceflight operations, with a focus on planning, scheduling, and timeline-execution tools for astronauts and mission controllers. Studies crew autonomy and self-scheduling as operational concepts for long-duration, high-latency deep-space missions, including the design and evaluation of the Playbook web-based scheduling tool. Investigates human-automation and human-robot interaction, usability assessment, and crew performance in isolated, confined, and extreme environments using analog missions such as HERA and NEEMO. Methods span usability and human performance studies, qualitative field observation (including remote conversation analysis), and human capability assessment for autonomous operations. Application domains center on extravehicular activity (EVA) planning, mission operations, and decision support for Moon-to-Mars exploration.
notes: Identification confidence: high. Anchored by the four pre-verified Human Factors papers (all authored by Marquez at NASA Ames). Identity and affiliation confirmed via NASA profile (nasa.gov/people/jessica-marquez), San Jose State University Women in Engineering bio, ResearchGate, NASA NTRS, and consistent co-authorship (Karasinski, Stirling, Lee, Shelat, Landon, Salas) across sources. She is the NASA Ames Human Systems Integration Division researcher leading the Playbook scheduling-tool team; PhD MIT 2007. All five non-HF DOIs verified via Crossref on 2026-06-17 with matching titles and author lists: HERA Campaign 7 (10.1177/10711813251370737, 2025), HCAAM/Autonomous Missions (10.1177/10711813251366310, 2025), Collaborative System Usability (10.3390/app14052005, 2024), EVA planning (10.1089/ast.2018.1838, 2019), Self-scheduling (10.1109/AERO.2017.7943838, 2017). Citation counts are Crossref is-referenced-by-count as of 2026-06-17: seminal_1 ~24, seminal_2 ~23 (both exceed the HF papers' counts, so HF papers were not selected as seminal). The two 2025 HFES Proceedings recent papers currently show 0 Crossref citations (expected for recent conference proceedings). No unverifiable citations were included; many of her NASA NTRS conference items lack DOIs and were excluded in favor of DOI-bearing works.
